coredata.dc:title: Long-lived ratiometric phosphorescence probe and portable device for cholesterol sensing in serum without background fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yawen
Jiang, Qianqian
Liang, Wanqing
Zhang, Cheng
Zhang, Kui
Zhang, Zhongping
coredata.dc:description: Visual detection using phosphorescence emission can effectively overcome the background fluorescence interference of complex samples, providing higher sensitivity and accuracy. However, the acquisition of phosphorescence photographs requires the recording of luminescence information immediately after ceasing the excitation source, which poses challenges for portable platforms. In this work, we demonstrated the quantitative analysis of cholesterol in serum by utilizing a homemade portable device combined with long-lived phosphorescent N and P doped carbon dots (NPCDs). The green emissive NPCDs were coated with silica and modified with β-cyclodextrin, resulting in an extremely long lifetime of 1.3 s and a special surface recognition function. By employing Rhodamine B as an energy acceptor, a triplet to singlet Förster resonance energy transfer system was established, leading to the transformation of green phosphorescence into orange-red phosphorescence. When cholesterol is present in the system, it disrupts the energy transfer process, resulting in the recovery of green phosphorescence. This unique color evolution without background fluorescence interference can be easily recorded using a portable device. Combined with RGB color analysis, this method enables the quantitative detection of cholesterol. This study provides a promising solution for the development of low-background nanoprobes and portable detection techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Ratiometric probe
Cholesterol
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and β-cyclodextrin modification of phosphorescent NPCDs@SiO2
Assembling of the ratiometric probe of RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2
The RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 for ratiometric phosphorescent detection of CHO
Determination of CHO in human serum samples
Determination of CHO on a portable phosphorescent image acquisition device
Synthesis and optical properties of NPCDs@SiO2
Assembling of the phosphorescent ratiometric probe
CHO analysis on a portable device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the long-lived probe for visual detection without interference of background fluorescence; (b) Schematic diagram of the ratiometric phosphorescence probe for the detection of CHO.
(a) TEM image of NPCDs@SiO2; (b) 3D phosphorescence spectra of NPCDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion; (c) phosphorescence decay curves (emission at 510 nm with 350 nm excitation) of NPCDs@SiO2 dispersion.

(a) Schematic diagram of the β-CD functionalized NPCDs@SiO2; (b) FT-IR spectra of NPCDs@SiO2 and β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2; (c) 3D phosphorescence spectra of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion; (d) phosphorescence emission spectra of NPCDs@SiO2 and β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2, inset: photographs of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 dispersion after turning off the 365 nm excitation light.

(a) Spectral overlap between the emission of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 and the absorption of RB; (b) phosphorescence spectra of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 after adding different concentrations of RB; (c) phosphorescence spectra of NPCDs@SiO2 after adding different concentrations of RB; (d) schematic diagram of the TS-FRET in the ratiometric probe.

(a) Phosphorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric probe dispersion with different concentrations of CHO; (b) the relationship between phosphorescence intensity ratio of I510/I576 and the concentration of CHO; (c) ratiometric phosphorescence response of the probe to CHO and interferents.

(a) Illustration of the homemade portable phosphorescent image acquisition device; (b) the fluorescence emission spectrum of human serum (inset shows the fluorescence color under 365 nm UV irradiation); (c) images of serum and serum + probe mixture taken by the portable device; (d) device obtained images of probe dispersion with addition of different concentrations of CHO; (e) linearly fitted calibration curves of green channel/red channel (G/R) with different concentrations of CHO.
Detection of CHO in serum samples using proposed method.

head.ce:title: Long-lived ratiometric phosphorescence probe and portable device for cholesterol sensing in serum without background fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, Anhui 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, Anhui 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visual detection using phosphorescence emission can effectively overcome the background fluorescence interference of complex samples, providing higher sensitivity and accuracy. However, the acquisition of phosphorescence photographs requires the recording of luminescence information immediately after ceasing the excitation source, which poses challenges for portable platforms. In this work, we demonstrated the quantitative analysis of cholesterol in serum by utilizing a homemade portable device combined with long-lived phosphorescent N and P doped carbon dots (NPCDs). The green emissive NPCDs were coated with silica and modified with β-cyclodextrin, resulting in an extremely long lifetime of 1.3 s and a special surface recognition function. By employing Rhodamine B as an energy acceptor, a triplet to singlet Förster resonance energy transfer system was established, leading to the transformation of green phosphorescence into orange-red phosphorescence. When cholesterol is present in the system, it disrupts the energy transfer process, resulting in the recovery of green phosphorescence. This unique color evolution without background fluorescence interference can be easily recorded using a portable device. Combined with RGB color analysis, this method enables the quantitative detection of cholesterol. This study provides a promising solution for the development of low-background nanoprobes and portable detection techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The luminescent ratiometric probe is a sensitive optical analysis technique that relies on two or more emission peaks [1,2]. One of the emission peaks is responsible for the spectral response of the analyte, such as luminescence enhancement or quenching, whereas the other serves as an internal reference signal [3,4]. By correlating the ratio of the luminescence intensities at these two different wavelengths with the concentration of the analyte, interference from factors such as probe concentration, excitation source fluctuations, and local environmental changes can be effectively eliminated [5]. The ratiometric method improves the sensitivity and accuracy of optical detection. Additionally, the relative intensity changes of two emissions can lead to significant output color changes [6,7]. The wide range of color changes provides convenience for visual detection and portable detection devices [8,9].

Unfortunately, luminescent ratiometric probes still face technical challenges that prevent the improvement of their analytical performance. First, a hybrid or covalent connection of two luminescent materials is typically used to achieve two emission peaks [10,11]. How to effectively excite the two luminescent materials simultaneously using a single excitation wavelength remains a great challenge. Second, currently reported luminescent ratiometric probes use short-lived fluorescent materials as signal units, such as organic dyes [12], fluorescent nanoclusters [13], quantum dots [14], etc. The primary drawback is the interference of background fluorescence generated by light excitation [15]. In particular, during visual detection, the resolution of the output colors is reduced. Therefore, exploring long-lived luminescent materials is a promising option. In recent years, room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots (RTP-CDs) have drawn wide attention from researchers, as they can be easily prepared and have an ultralong lifetimes [16,17]. Doping heterogeneous atoms into carbon dots can enhance spin-orbit coupling, which is conducive to the generation of excited triplet states through intersystem crossing (ISC) [15,18,19]. On the other hand, immobilization of the carbon dots in the matrix can inhibit the non-radiative transition pathway of the excited triplet state, leading to room-temperature phosphorescent emission of carbon dots with durations of several seconds [20,21].

Cholesterol (CHO) is a sterol synthesized by animal cells and its main function is to maintain the integrity and fluidity of cell membranes [22,23]. CHO is also a synthetic precursor of bile acids, steroid hormones, etc. Chemical imbalance of CHO can easily lead to cardiovascular diseases such as atherosclerotic plaques [24]. Recent studies have shown that CHO metabolism can achieve a series of intracellular regulatory effects, thus playing an important role in the occurrence and development of tumorigenesis [25]. Therefore, it is of great significance to effectively detect CHO content. Traditional CHO detection methods include liquid chromatography electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry (LC-ESI-MS/MS) [26], electrochemical analysis [27], chemiluminescence (ECL) [28], colorimetry [29], and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)[30]. Notably, CHO detection based on luminescent ratiometric probes has also been developed [31–33]. However, these probes still rely on short-lived fluorescent signals with background interference. In addition, most of these probes work with the help of cholesterol oxidase, which increases the cost and complexity of the assay.

Herein, we proposed a ratiometric method for the visual detection of CHO by using long-lived RTP-CDs. N and P doped carbon dots (NPCDs) coated with silica (NPCDs@SiO2) exhibit excellent dispersibility in water and persistent phosphorescence at room temperature, which can overcome the interference of background fluorescence ( Fig. 1a). In addition, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) modification was performed on the surface of NPCDs@SiO2 to achieve efficient CHO recognition. β-CD modified NPCDs@SiO2 interacts with rhodamine B (RB) to form a ratiometric probe based on triplet to singlet Förster resonance energy transfer (TS-FRET). When CHO is present, it can break the TS-FRET process because of the affinity between CHO and β-CD (Fig. 1b). On this basis, the quantitative detection of CHO was realized. Since the output color variation is noticeable from orange-red to green, CHO in serum can be analyzed more easily on a portable phosphorescent photo acquisition apparatus without background fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and β-cyclodextrin modification of phosphorescent NPCDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: N and P doped CDs and water-dispersible NPCDs@SiO2 were synthesized and purified as previously reported [34]. To prepare β-CD functionalized NPCDs@SiO2, 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (IPTS) was used to link β-CD and NPCDs@SiO2 covalently. First, NPCDs@SiO2 were completely dispersed in DMF (approximately 10 mg/mL). Then, 0.1155 g of β-CD was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF in a round-bottomed flask. After that, 40 μL of IPTS was added under stirring, and the flask was placed in a temperature-controlled magnetic stirrer at 60 °C and stirred for 6 h. Subsequently, 10 mL of the NPCDs@SiO2 DMF dispersion was added, and the mixed solution was stirred continuously and reacted at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling naturally, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with DMF and water three times to remove unreacted substances. Finally, the product β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 was re-dispersed in water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembling of the ratiometric probe of RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the ratiometric probe, 70 μL of rhodamine B (RB) solution (500 μM) was added to 5 mL of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion (2.0 mg/mL), and the mixture was incubated at 30 °C for 3 h. Eventually, the mixture was centrifuged and redispersed in 5 mL of ultrapure water to obtain the RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 for ratiometric phosphorescent detection of CHO
ce:section.ce:para: CHO standard stock solution (20 mM) was prepared according to a previous report[35]. Typically, 38.7 mg of powdered CHO was added with 0.5 mL of TritonX-100 (98 %) and a specific amount of water, and the mixture was heated at 165 °C for 5 min to ensure complete dissolution of CHO. Finally, the above solution was fixed to 5 mL with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use. For ratiometric detection of CHO, 200 μL of the RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 probe dispersion was added to various volumes of CHO solution in the cuvette and diluted with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 20 mM, pH=7.4) to a total volume of 1 mL. After incubation at 37 °C in the dark, phosphorescence spectra were recorded by excitation at 350 nm using a luminescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CHO in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The collected human serum samples were diluted with PBS (20 mM, pH=7.4) and stored in a refrigerator for future use. To measure the concentration of CHO in serum, 200 μL of the RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 probe and diluted serum were mixed in PBS (20 mM, pH=7.4) buffer to a total volume of 1 mL. The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 5 min. The phosphorescence spectra were recorded by excitation at 350 nm using a luminescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CHO on a portable phosphorescent image acquisition device
ce:section.ce:para: The homemade phosphorescent image acquisition device mainly consisted of a 365 nm excitation light source, a rotatable shutter, and a digital camera. Using this device, phosphorescent photographs could be easily obtained without fluorescence signals. Typically, 200 μL of the RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 probe and CHO samples were mixed in PBS (20 mM, pH=7.4) buffer to a total volume of 1 mL. After incubation at 37 °C in the dark for 5 min, the mixture was transferred to 24 well plate. Then, the well plate was placed in the portable device, and the phosphorescence photos were taken with a Canon 600D digital camera (aperture was F5.6, and exposure time was 13 s). Finally, the RGB values of the phosphorescence photos were analyzed to quantify the CHO content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of NPCDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The NPCDs and NPCDs@SiO2 were prepared as previously reported [34]. Owing to the use of ethanolamine and phosphoric acid as precursors, effective doping with N and P can be achieved, facilitating the ISC process to produce excited triplet states under UV light. The morphology, elemental composition, and chemical bonding are presented in Fig. S1 and S2. When the NPCDs were coated with silica (NPCDs@SiO2), triplet state quenching of external factors on the NPCDs was effectively prevented, generating long-lived phosphorescence emission. As shown in Fig. 2a, the prepared NPCDs@SiO2 presented uniformly distributed near-spherical particles with a particle size of approximately 7.58 nm (Fig. S3a). The UV–vis absorption of NPCDs exhibited two distinct absorption peaks, which were centered at approximately 266 nm and 330 nm. These peaks could be respectively ascribed to the π-π* transition and the n-π* transition (Fig. S3b). The phosphorescence spectra show that under optimal excitation of 350 nm, NPCDs@SiO2 produced an emission peak at 510 nm, and the position of the emission peak did not change with variation of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b). The luminescence decay curve shows that the phosphorescence lifetime of NPCDs@SiO2 at 510 nm was 1504.08 ms (Fig. 2c and Table S1). Therefore, NPCDs@SiO2 is an ideal candidate for phosphorescent probes.

The surface of the NPCDs@SiO2 was modified with β-CD to realize a ratiometric luminescence strategy. β-CD is a cyclic oligosaccharide consisting of seven glucose units that selectively recognize oil-soluble molecules and form inclusion complexes with them. As shown in Fig. 3a, the presence of the hydroxyl group (-OH) in β-CD enabled its interaction with IPTS. Based on this, β-CD functionalized IPTS was first prepared, and then the hydrolysis of silane was used to achieve β-CD functionalization of the surface of NPCDs@SiO2. TEM revealed that the size of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 was 8.50 nm (Fig. S4). The particle size increased slightly relative to that before modification. The successful modification of β-CD was further confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization of the NPCDs@SiO2 samples before and after the modification (Fig. 3b). In the modified sample, the emergence of a new characteristic peak at 2930 cm⁻¹ could be ascribed to the vibration of C-H bonds, which constituted the principal evidence for the successful modification of β-CD[36]. The characteristic absorption peak at 1543 cm⁻¹ in the modified sample was attributed to the bending vibration of N-H bonds. Additionally, characteristic absorption peaks of amide bonds were detected at 1640 and 1380 cm⁻¹ in the modified sample. The characteristic C-H stretching vibration originating from β-CD and the characteristic absorption peaks of amide bonds formed during the modification process signified that β-CD/NPCDs@SiO₂ had been successfully fabricated.

It has been demonstrated that modification of β-CD does not lead to a noticeable difference in the long-lived luminescence of NPCDs@SiO2. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the emission peak was observed at 510 nm when subjected to an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Additionally, the emission peak position remained unchanged at 510 nm even when the excitation wavelength was varied from 300 to 400 nm. The above results further confirm that the phosphorescence originates from the NPCDs, and that the NPCDs have been efficiently immobilized in the silica matrix. Therefore, β-CD modification did not affect the emission peak positions of NPCDs (Fig. 3d). The phosphorescence luminescence lifetime became 1302.50 ms after modification (Table S1), and the slight lifetime decrease may originate from the fact that the radiative emission from the modified β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 is faster than that in the NPCDs@SiO2, and more photons decayed before significant radiation occurred [37]. Even so, the modified β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 maintained the naked eye visible phosphorescence for approximately 8 s (inset of Fig. 3d). Thus, the present material also offers the possibility of constructing visual probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembling of the phosphorescent ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: To construct a ratiometric luminescence probe, RB was combined with β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 to realize double emission peaks of the probe. RB was chosen based on the following points: i) the emission peak of RB was located in the red light region, with a large shift between the green phosphorescence of the NPCDs. The difference between the red and green luminescence signals facilitated the visualization; ii) the excitation of RB was located in the green region, and the simultaneous occurrence of green and red emissions under a single wavelength excitation can be realized by the energy transfer between NPCDs and RB; iii) there is a host-guest interaction between RB and β-CD [38], which can be more easily realized the probe assembly.

To verify the above perspectives, the emission spectra of the NPCDs and the absorption spectra of RB were compared. As shown in Fig. 4a, the maximum absorption of RB was located at 553 nm, and there was a good spectral overlap with the phosphorescence emission peak of NPCDs@SiO2. When different concentrations of RB were introduced into the dispersion of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2, the intensity of the green phosphorescence at 510 nm incrementally declined, while the emission of RB at 576 nm gradually increased (Fig. 4b). As a control test, the RB signal at 576 nm was found to be extremely weak when introduced into unmodified NPCDs@SiO2 (Fig. 4c). By plotting the intensity ratio of I510/I576 against the amount of RB, it was found that the ratio of β-CD modified probe decreased by 87 %, while that of the unmodified sample only decreased by 43 % when 7 μM of RB was present (Fig. S5). The above results confirm that β-CD modification played an important role and that the RB assembly was saturated when the addition reached 7 μM.

To deeply explore the origin of RB luminescence, the fluorescence spectra of RB and the phosphorescence spectra of the probe RB-β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 were compared. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence emission peak of RB was located at 576 nm, suggesting that the emission of RB in the ratiometric probe originated from the radiative transition of a singlet excited state. The phosphorescence decay curves showed that the lifetime of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 at 510 nm gradually decreased with the addition of RB (Fig. S7 and Table S2). This can be attributed to the nonradiative energy transfer between NPCDs and RB [34]. Based on the decrease in lifetime, the energy transfer efficiency was estimated to be 44.14 % after incorporation of 7 μM RB.

Based on the above results, the luminescence mechanism of the probe can be explained as follows (Fig. 4d). UV light causes the NPCDs to transition from the singlet ground state to the excited singlet state, and the ISC between the excited singlet state and the excited triplet state is effectively triggered by the doping of N and P. Simultaneously, the protection of the silica matrix actually inhibits the quenching of the triplet excited state. Consequently, phosphorescence of the NPCDs was generated at 510 nm. When RB was present, RB formed an inclusion complex with β-CD, which was modified on the surface of the probe. Therefore, RB remains close enough to the NPCDs (<10 nm), and there is an effective spectral overlap between RB and NPCDs, so that Förster resonance energy transfer between the triplet state of NPCDs and the singlet state of RB can occur. Finally, RB activated by the energy transfer process produces a radiative transition (576 nm) from the singlet excited state to the singlet ground state. Therefore, under a single wavelength excitation, the simultaneous occurrence of long-lived green and red emissions can be achieved.

The sufficiently close distance between NPCDs and RB in the probes is a necessary condition for the generation of the TS-FRET process, which is achieved through the host-guest interaction of β-CD and RB. Inspired by competitive inclusion, when CHO was present in the system, there was a stronger affinity interaction between CHO and β-CD with higher binding constants [23,30]. As a result, RB detached from the surface of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2, thus disrupting the TS-FRET process (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 5a, with the increase of CHO content in the system, the red emission at 576 nm gradually diminished, and the green emission at 510 nm gradually recovered. Using the intensity ratio of the two emission peaks plotted against CHO concentration, it was found that there was a good linear relationship between the two in a certain concentration range (Fig. 5b). The linear ranges were 10 μM-0.2 mM and 0.25 mM-0.50 mM, respectively. Based on the signal-to-noise ratio, the detection limit of the method was calculated to be 1.13 μM. The proposed method has high sensitivity compared to the CHO detection methods reported in the literature (Table S3).

Furthermore, an in-depth analysis of the response mechanism of the probe to CHO was conducted. When CHO was introduced into the dispersion of β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 or RB solution, respectively, there was no variation in the luminescence signal, indicating that CHO had no effect on the phosphorescence of NPCDs and the fluorescence of RB (Fig. S8). Furthermore, the influence of CHO on phosphorescence lifetime of ratiometric probe was tested (Fig. S9 and Table S4). The results showed that the addition of CHO restored the lifetime of the phosphorescence donor at 510 nm, further confirming that CHO disrupted the TS-FRET process. Time-dependent experiments demonstrated that the response of the probe to CHO is a very rapid process, with a marked response after 4 min of adding CHO (Fig. S10). A stable signal was achieved after 5 min. Consequently, in the CHO sensing, it was deemed appropriate to set the incubation time to 5 min.

The modification of β-CD not only facilitated the energy transfer process but also enabled selective detection of CHO. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, various inorganic ions (such as Na+, K+, and Mg2+) and organic molecules (including glycine, tryptophan, aspartate, alanine, citrulline, glutamate, leucine, arginine, ascorbic acid, lecithin, glucose, glutathione, and urea) elicited no response from the probe. This phenomenon can be attributed to the high affinity of CHO molecules for β-CD. As a result, the method was used for the determination of CHO in human serum samples with recoveries ranging from 94.92 % to 97.19 %, indicating that the method can be applied to the analysis of real samples ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CHO analysis on a portable device
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed ratiometric phosphorescence methodology exhibits the capacity not only to precisely detect CHO through a spectrophotometer but also to conduct visual detection platform. Nevertheless, when implementing the phosphorescence approach for visual detection, two principal difficulties are encountered. Firstly, although the phosphorescence method can effectively eliminate background fluorescence, it is requisite to record the emission image subsequent to the cessation of UV excitation light. Secondly, given that the luminescence signal decays over time [39], if the luminescence signal within the same decay time cannot be recorded accurately, it may cause an inconsistency in brightness.

To surmount these challenges, a portable device was devised to capture the phosphorescence color. This device predominantly comprises an excitation light source emitting at 365 nm, a rotatable shutter, and a digital camera ( Fig. 6a). The UV light originating from the light source initially illuminated on the sample under unobstructed conditions. When the shutter is positioned atop the light source, the excitation light is obstructed, preventing irradiation of samples. As the shutter rotates under motor propulsion, the sample can be exposed to UV light, thereby eliciting luminescence. Owing to the uniform rotational speed of the shutter driven by the motor, periodic excitation of the sample by the UV light is achieved.

The phosphorescence probe possesses a long lifetime, enabling the phosphorescence to endure for approximately 8 s following excitation cessation (Fig. 3d). In contrast, the background fluorescence signal (Fig. 6b) has an extremely short lifetime, such that it can be postulated that the fluorescence dissipates instantaneously upon the termination of excitation light irradiation. Consequently, an emission shutter is installed above the sample. During sample irradiation by the excitation light, the emission shutter is positioned in front of the camera, precluding the detection of any light signal. Upon shutter rotation, the excitation light is blocked and the fluorescence extinguishes. However, the phosphorescence persisting within the detection system can be efficaciously recorded by the camera. For verification purposes, serum with green fluorescence could not be discerned under this device, whereas serum mixed with the probe could be photographed with a distinct luminescent color (Fig. 6c).

With the help of this device, it is easy to record photos of the phosphorescence color change of the probe in response to different concentrations of CHO. As shown in the Fig. 6d, when different concentrations of CHO were added to the probe solution, the phosphorescence color of the probe gradually changed from orange-red to green, further proving that the addition of CHO disrupted the energy transfer process between the NPCDs and RB. Using the naked eye to observe color changes, it is possible to assess the level of CHO content. Furthermore, we performed an R-G-B numerical analysis of the images. There was a good linear relationship between the ratio of the green channel to the red channel (G/R) and the CHO concentration (Fig. 6e), which can be used for the quantitative detection of CHO. Finally, human serum samples were added to the probe solution and phosphorescence color were analyzed. The CHO levels in serum samples were successfully detected (Table S5), demonstrating the convenience and practicality of this method.

In conclusion, a ratiometric phosphorescent probe was fabricated through energy transfer between NPCDs and RB at room temperature to achieve the sensitive detection of cholesterol in serum. Heteroatom doping and silica protection endow NPCDs@SiO2 with an extremely long lifetime in aqueous solution, and cyclodextrin modification enables β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 to display binding capacity to RB. An energy transfer system with β-CD/NPCDs@SiO2 as the energy donor and RB as the energy acceptor was established within the probe. The binding of CHO to β-CD disrupts the energy transfer process of the probe, and the probe exhibits a color transformation from orange-red to green at varying concentrations of cholesterol. The determination of serum CHO without background fluorescence interference was achieved via phosphorescence color analysis using a homemade photographic acquisition device equipped with a rotating shutter. This work provides opportunities to develop highly sensitive and convenient on-site detection methods.

Gao Yawen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiang Qianqian: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Liang Wanqing: Validation, Formal analysis. Zhang Cheng: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhang Kui: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Zhang Zhongping: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22106005, 22474003), the Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (2022AH030047, 2022AH010023), and Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2408085JX004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric optical nanoprobes enable accurate molecular detection and imaging
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Huang, J. Song, B.C. Yung, X. Huang, Y. Xiong, X. Chen, Ratiometric optical nanoprobes enable accurate molecular detection and imaging, Chem. Soc. Rev. 47 (2018) 2873-2920.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zn-CD@Eu ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of dipicolinic acid, uric acid, and ex vivo uric acid imaging
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Mohapatro, L. Mishra, M. Mishra, S. Mohapatra, Zn-CD@Eu ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of dipicolinic acid, uric acid, and ex vivo uric acid imaging, Anal. Chem. 96 (2024) 8630-8640.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of ratiometric aggregation-induced emission luminogens for biosensing and bioimaging
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, Q. Yang, X. Lv, Y. Xiong, B.Z. Tang, X. Huang, Rational design of ratiometric aggregation-induced emission luminogens for biosensing and bioimaging, Coord. Chem. Rev. 516 (2024) 215970.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical control of fluorescence lifetime towards multiplexing imaging
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yawen Gao is currently pursuing her M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. Her current field of research interest is luminescent sensor.
Qianqian Jiang is currently pursuing her M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. Her current field of research interest is carbon dots.
Wanqing Liang is currently pursuing his M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. His current field of research interest is carbon dots.

Cheng Zhang is associate professor in Anhui University of Technology. He got a PhD from University of Science and Technology of China in 2017. His current research interests are focused on the design and synthesis of luminescent materials for environmental and sensing applications.

Kui Zhang is professor at Anhui University of Technology in China. He got a PhD from University of Science and Technology of China in 2011. His research interests are in analytical chemistry, nanotechnology, and the development of functional nanomaterials for chemo/bio sensing, environmental and energy application.

Zhongping Zhang is currently a full distinguished professor at the Anhui University. His research interests span from molecular engineering of nanomaterial surfaces to nanosensors and visual assay/imaging.
